statement: For a nominal compact-disc or CD-derived PCM audio stream, the sampling frequency is concentrated at the 44.1 kHz standard; this Normal prior represents small clock and nominal-rate uncertainty around the standard rather than variation across all audio formats.
source: IEC 60908 Compact disc digital audio system standard; Pohlmann, Principles of Digital Audio

statement: For diagnostic electrocardiogram acquisition, digitizers are commonly operated around 500 Hz, with nearby standard choices such as 250 Hz and 1000 Hz also encountered; the LogNormal parameters are on the natural-log scale and center the prior at 500 Hz.
source: IEC 60601-2-25 electrocardiograph standard; Webster, Medical Instrumentation: Application and Design

statement: For industrial rotating-machinery vibration monitoring intended to capture bearing and gear fault content, sample rates in the tens of kilohertz are common; this LogNormal prior is centered at 25.6 kHz using natural-log-scale parameters.
source: Randall, Vibration-based Condition Monitoring; ISO 18431 mechanical vibration signal-processing standards

statement: For a first-order analog RC low-pass or anti-alias pole placed near the audio-band edge of about 20 kHz, the corresponding time constant is approximately 1/(2*pi*20000) = 7.96 microseconds; this LogNormal prior uses natural-log-scale parameters to allow component tolerance and modest design variation.
source: Horowitz & Hill, The Art of Electronics; Oppenheim & Schafer, Discrete-Time Signal Processing

statement: For industrial process transmitters and measurement channels with configurable first-order damping, a time constant on the order of 1 second is common; this Gamma prior uses PyMC's shape-rate parameterization with mean 1.0 s and standard deviation about 0.67 s.
source: Liptak, Instrument Engineers' Handbook; Doebelin, Measurement Systems: Application and Design

statement: For a discrete-time anti-alias or decimation low-pass design, the passband or effective cutoff is typically placed below Nyquist with transition-band margin; this Beta prior has mean 0.4 for the cutoff expressed as a fraction of the Nyquist frequency.
source: Oppenheim & Schafer, Discrete-Time Signal Processing; Crochiere & Rabiner, Multirate Digital Signal Processing

statement: For an ideal 12-bit ADC converting a full-scale sine wave with uncorrelated quantization noise, the canonical quantization-limited SNR is 6.02*N + 1.76 dB, giving about 74.0 dB; this Normal prior is expressed directly in dB space.
source: Kester, The Data Conversion Handbook; IEEE Std 1241 terminology and test methods for analog-to-digital converters

statement: For an open-sky GPS L1 C/A receiver with a consumer-to-survey-grade antenna, tracked carrier-to-noise-density ratios commonly lie in the high 30s to high 40s dB-Hz; this Normal prior is centered at 43 dB-Hz.
source: Kaplan & Hegarty, Understanding GPS/GNSS: Principles and Applications

statement: For coherent uncoded BPSK in an additive white Gaussian noise channel, the textbook bit-error-rate relation gives a required Eb/N0 of about 9.6 dB at BER = 1e-5; the Normal prior allows small implementation and convention variation in dB space.
source: Proakis & Salehi, Digital Communications; Sklar, Digital Communications: Fundamentals and Applications

statement: For a well-designed high-speed data-converter sampling clock, RMS aperture jitter is often on the sub-picosecond to picosecond scale and directly limits high-frequency SNR; this HalfNormal prior uses a 1 ps scale and enforces nonnegative jitter.
source: Kester, The Data Conversion Handbook; Analog Devices data-converter application notes on aperture jitter

statement: For FFT spectral estimation using a Hann window, the equivalent noise bandwidth is canonically about 1.5 DFT-bin widths; this Gamma prior uses PyMC's shape-rate parameterization and has mean 1.5 bins with standard deviation 0.05 bins for finite-length and convention uncertainty.
source: Lyons, Understanding Digital Signal Processing; Harris, classic reference on FFT window functions
